coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of silver nanocrystal with an excellent oxidase-like activity and its application in colorimetric detection of D-penicillamine
coredata.dc:creator: Dan, Xu
Ruiyi, Li
Qinsheng, Wang
Yongqiang, Yang
Guangli, Wang
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: High-index facets of metal nanocrystal have often a higher catalytic activity compared with the low-index facets. However, the shape-controlled synthesis of silver nanocrystal still faces great challenges. In the study, we reported synthesis of silver nanocrystals using histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQDs) as a chelating agent and shape inducer. Silver ions were coordinated with His-GQDs to form stable complex and then heated at 180 °C for 3 h. The resulting Ag/His-GQD nanohybrid offers polygonal nanostructure. The architecture improves the exposure of high-index facets, leading to an improved oxidase-like activity. Ag/His-GQD was used as the mimetic enzyme for colorimetric detection of D-penicillamine. In the detection Ag/His-GQD catalyzes the oxidization of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine into blue product. D-penicillamine can inhibit the catalytic activity of Ag/His-GQD and leads to a reduced oxidization rate, because sulfhydryl in D-penicillamine was combined with silver in the Ag/His-GQD to form Ag-S bond. The absorbance at 652 nm linearly decreases with increasing D-penicillamine concentration in the range from 0.1 to 1.25 μM with detection limit of 0.034 μM (S/N = 3). The proposed method exhibits a better sensitivity, selectivity and stability and was successfully applied in colorimetric detection of D-penicillamine. The study also provides a promising approach for synthesis of metal nanocrystals with excellent catalytic activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal nano-enzyme
Functionalized graphene quantum dots
Hybrid
D-penicillamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Inhibitory effect of D-PA on the oxidase-like activity of Ag/His-GQD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis scheme (A), SEM image (B), TEM image (C), HRTEM image (D) of Ag/His-GQD, XRD patterns (E) of Ag/His-GQD and H-Ag/His-GQD.

UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag/His-GQD + TMB (a), Ag/His-GQD (b), TMB (c). B: UV–vis absorption spectra of the TMB oxidation catalyzed by Ag/His-GQD (a), H-Ag/His-GQD (b), spherical Ag (c) and His-GQD (d).

A: Schematic diagram of the Ag/His-GQD as an mimetic enzyme for the colorimetric detection of D-PA. B: UV–vis absorption spectra of the reaction system with D-PA concentration ranging from 0.1 to 1.25 μM. C: The relationship curve between ΔA and D-PA concentration.
The A652 values of reaction system added 0.5 μM D-PA in the presence of 0.5 μM of sucrose, lactose, glucose, starch, SDS, K+, Na+ and Ca2+.
Comparison of the Km and νmax values of Ag/His-GQD with those of other oxidase enzyme.
Sensitivity comparison of different analytical techniques for D-PA detection.
The results of determination of D-PA in medicine samples (N = 3).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of silver nanocrystal with an excellent oxidase-like activity and its application in colorimetric detection of D-penicillamine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine Hanlin College, Taizhou 225300, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-index facets of metal nanocrystal have often a higher catalytic activity compared with the low-index facets. However, the shape-controlled synthesis of silver nanocrystal still faces great challenges. In the study, we reported synthesis of silver nanocrystals using histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQDs) as a chelating agent and shape inducer. Silver ions were coordinated with His-GQDs to form stable complex and then heated at 180 °C for 3 h. The resulting Ag/His-GQD nanohybrid offers polygonal nanostructure. The architecture improves the exposure of high-index facets, leading to an improved oxidase-like activity. Ag/His-GQD was used as the mimetic enzyme for colorimetric detection of D-penicillamine. In the detection Ag/His-GQD catalyzes the oxidization of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine into blue product. D-penicillamine can inhibit the catalytic activity of Ag/His-GQD and leads to a reduced oxidization rate, because sulfhydryl in D-penicillamine was combined with silver in the Ag/His-GQD to form Ag-S bond. The absorbance at 652 nm linearly decreases with increasing D-penicillamine concentration in the range from 0.1 to 1.25 μM with detection limit of 0.034 μM (S/N = 3). The proposed method exhibits a better sensitivity, selectivity and stability and was successfully applied in colorimetric detection of D-penicillamine. The study also provides a promising approach for synthesis of metal nanocrystals with excellent catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:para: With rapid development of nanotechnology, various nanomaterials were widely used as the metal nanoenzyme for environmental protection, biosensing, antibacterial and cancer treatment [1–4]. Nanoenzyme gives a similar catalytic mechanism to natural enzymes [5]. Unlike natural enzymes, nanoenzymes mostly are low-cost, easy to prepare, and can maintain stable catalytic activity under harsh conditions [6–7]. More importantly, the catalytic activity of nanoenzymes can be easily regulated by controlling size, morphology, structure and surface modification of nanoparticles. These unique advantages make nanoenzymes have a better application prospects. The reported nanozymes are mainly focus on noble metal nanoparticles (Pt, Pd, Ag), metal oxides (Fe3O4, CeO2, Co3O4) and their derivatives [8–12]. Due to low cost, excellent optical and electrical properties and biocompatibility, Ag nanometals have received a more attention in electronics, photonics, catalyst and biological industry [13]. However, Ag nanoparticles often offer a relatively low catalytic activity compared with natural enzymes. This greatly limits its further applications. Therefore, the development of an effective method for synthesis of Ag nanocrystal with excellent catalytic activity is of importance.

The catalytic activity of nanomaterials is often affected by their crystal structure, morphology, particle size and specific surface [14]. Therefore, the controlling synthesis of Ag nanocrystals is the key to obtain high catalytic activity. In the recent years, several strategies have been developed to enhance the catalytic activity of Ag nanoparticles. The first strategy is to improve the dispersion of Ag nanoparticles, which can effectively increase the specific surface area. The exposure of more active sites leads to the improvement of catalytic activity. Hussain et al. reported synthesis Ag nanoparticles with good dispersibility and stability by using monoethanolamine as the reducing agent and poly(acrylic acid) as the stabilizing agent. The Ag nanoparticles show a high catalytic activity towards methylene blue and p-nitrophenol [15]. The second strategy is to reduce size of Ag nanoparticles. For the small-sized nanoparticles, the atom utilization rate is increased due to the increase in surface atom occupancy, thereby improving the catalytic activity. Maddinedi et al. prepared Ag nanoparticles with different particle sizes (13 nm, 22 nm and 33 nm) by using tyrosine as reductant and stabilizer. The results show that Ag nanoparticles with particle size of 13 nm have a higher catalytic activity towards 4-nitrophenol [16]. The third strategy is to make the Ag nanoparticles with a porous structure. This structure can provide a higher specific surface area and porosity and bring more surface reaction sites or interfacial interaction, thereby improving the catalytic activity. Lu et al. prepared a porous Ag material, which was used to selectively catalyze carbon dioxide into carbon monoxide [17]. The results show that the activity of porous Ag is 20 times higher than that of polycrystalline Ag. The fourth strategy is to increase the exposure of high-index crystal faces of Ag nanocrystals. The high-index crystal faces contain a large number of step atoms and unpaired atoms, and has few coordination numbers and high chemical activity [18]. Hence, the high-index crystal faces exhibit a higher catalytic activity than the low-index crystal faces. Xu et al. reported synthesis of nanocube, triangular and near‐spherical Ag nanoparticles by PVP and hydrazine hydrate. Nanocube Ag exhibited higher catalytic activity than near‐spherical and nanoplates due to the exposure of {100} crystal face with higher surface energy [19]. Xia et al. reported synthesis of Ag concave octahedrons and trisoctahedrons, which gave SERS signals approximately 4 and 12 times stronger than the conventional octahedrons with a similar size, respectively [20]. However, the high exponential crystal surface often have a faster growth rate than the low-index crystal faces. As a result, high-index crystal faces will rapidly disappear in the growth process of nanocrystals and leaves the low-index crystal faces exposed on the surface of nanocrystals [21–22]. Therefore, synthesis of highly catalytic Ag nanocrystals is still a challenge.

The study reported synthesis of Ag nanocrystals using histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQDs) as a chelating agent and shape inducer. The as-synthesized Ag/His-GQD nanohybrid shows a polygonal structure. This unique architecture creates more exposure of high-index crystal facets and more active sites and greatly improves the oxidase-like activity of the Ag/His-GQD. Based on the inhibitory effect of D-penicillamine (D-PA) on the oxidase-like activity of Ag/His-GQD, one colorimetric method was developed for detection of D-PA. The proposed method provides the advantage of high sensitivity, selectivity and stability. It has been successfully applied to determination of D-PA.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3), histidine, citric acid, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), D-penicillamine (D-PA), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), p-benzoquinone, terephthalic acid, acetic acid, sodium acetate, sucrose, lactose, glucose, maltose, starch, sulphate (SDS), sodium chloride, potassium chloride and calcium chloride were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich company. All aqueous solutions were prepared with doubly distilled water, which was obtained from a Milli-Q water purifying system.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) measurements were conducted on HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurements were conducted on JEOL 2010 FEG microscope at 200 keV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on D8 Advance Instrument operated at 40 kV and 20 mA and using Cu Kα radiation source with λ = 0.15406 nm. Infrared spectrum (IR) was recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer. Fluorescence spectrums were measured by Cary Esclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on PHI 5700 ESCA electron spectrometer.

His-GQD was synthesized by according to the reference [23]. Typically, citric acid of 2.22 g and histidine of 1.85 g were mixed and dissolved in 2 mL of doubly distilled water. The mixture was heated at 180 °C for 3 h to obtain His-GQD. To prepare Ag/His-GQD, His-GQD (1.5 g) was dispersed in 50 mL doubly distilled water to form a uniform dispersion. Then, its acidity was adjusted to pH 7 by dropping 1 M NaOH aqueous solution. The AgNO3 (0.1 M, 10.0 mL) solution was slowly dropped into the above solution under stirring to form stable Ag/His-GQD complex. The formed complex was collected by centrifugation at 8000 r/min and then washed for three times with doubly distilled water. The obtained solid sample was re-dispersed into the distilled water and heated in Teflon lined autoclave at 180 °C for 3 h. Followed by centrifugation at 8000 r/min and washing with doubly distilled water for several times. The collected solid sample was dried in vacuum at 50 °C to obtain Ag/His-GQD. In addition, two Ag nanomaterials were also prepared, including spherical Ag/His-GQD and spherical Ag nanoparticles. The spherical Ag/His-GQD (H-Ag/His-GQD) was obtained by the reduction of Ag/His-GQD complex at 400 °C in N2 atmosphere for 3 h. The spherical Ag nanoparticles were synthesized by direct chemical reducrion method using sodium borohydride as a reducing agent instead of His-GQD [24].

TMB was used as a a chromogenic substrate to investigate the oxidase-like activity of Ag/His-GQD. Ag/His-GQD (200 μL, 2 mg mL−1) was added to the acetate buffer solution (700 μL, 0.1 M, pH 4), and then added 100 μL of TMB (4 mM). After thoroughly mixing, was incubated at 30 °C for 20 min. Finally, the absorption spectra were recorded by UV–vis spectrophotometer in range of 400–800 nm. The spherical Ag, H-Ag/His-GQD and His-GQDs were tested using the same procedure.

Different concentrations of TMB (1–5 mM, 100 μL) was added to the acetate buffer solution (900 μL, 0.1 M, pH 4) containing 0.4 mg of Ag/His-GQD. After mixing, the change in absorbance at 652 nm (A652) is recorded by UV–vis spectrophotometer. The Michaelis (Km) and the maximal reaction velocity (νmax) were calculated by Lineweaver-Burk plot as shown in formula (1), where [S] is the concentration of TMB and νmax is the maximum reaction velocity. ν is the reaction velocity, which was obtained by the Beer-Lambert law, the molar absorption coefficient of ox-TMB at 652 nm is 39000 M−1 cm−1.
(1)
1 ν
=
K
m
+ [ S ]
S
·
ν
max

The D-PA standard solution (100 μL) and acetate buffer solution (0.1 M, 600 μL, pH 4) were mixed with the Ag/His-GQD solution (2 mg mL−1, 200 μL). Added the TMB solution (4 mM, 100 μL) and incubated at 30 °C for 20 min. The mixed solution was transferred to a quartz test tube for spectral scanning or measureing absorbance at 652 nm.

Synthesis of Ag/His-GQD mainly involves two steps (Fig. 1 ). Firstly, Ag+ ion was reacted with His-GQD to form stable Ag/His-GQD complex and then heated at 180 °C for 3 h to obtain Ag/His-GQD. In the process, His-GQD plays important role. On the one hand, His-GQD acts as the chelating agent. The combination of Ag+ ions and His-GQDs is accomplished by the coordination of Ag+ ions with the nitrogen atom in the His-GQDs, which can improve the dispersion and stability of Ag+ ions. This way can effectively slow down the reduction speed during the reduction process of Ag+ to Ag0. Furthermore, this coordination way causes the polymerization of small graphene sheets of His-GQDs. With the increase of the degree of polymerization, the water-solubility of the Ag/His-GQD complex becomes worse. This leads to an increase of precipitation in the system (shown in Fig. s1). The binding of Ag+ ions to His-GQDs was also verified by fluorescence behavior. As shown in Fig. s2, the addition of Ag+ ions leads to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of His-GQDs. This is caused by the energy resonance transfer between His-GQDs and Ag+ ions. On the second hand, His-GQD acts as the reducing agent. The Ag-N bonds on Ag/His-GQD complex are slowly break to form Ag+ at high temperature. At the same time, the most of His-GQDs are released from the complex. At the same time, Ag+ ions was reduced to Ag0 and finally result in the formation of Ag nanoparticles. During the heating, the reserved His-GQDs were bound to the surface of Ag nanoparticles to form Ag/His-GQD nanohybrid. To confirm the release of His-GQDs, the fluorescence of the reaction system was monitored by Ultraviolet radiation. It can be seen from Fig. s3 that the solution changes from no blue to blue, indicating an increased fluorescence emission. This demonstrates that some His-GQDs were released from the complex to the aqueous solution during the heating process. In the synthesis, the released His-GQDs acts as a shape inducer. These His-GQDs can be selectively adsorbed on the surface of crystal faces of Ag nanocrystals. This will slow down the growth rate of Ag nanocrystals [25]. As different crystal face offers a different adsorption ability towards His-GQDs, the above adsorption behavior can achieve to controlled synthesis of Ag nanocrystals. This helps to improve exposure of high-index crystal faces of the Ag nanoparticles in the Ag/His-GQD.

The as-synthesized Ag/His-GQD was characterized by SEM, TEM, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), XRD. As shown in Fig. 1B and C, the most of Ag/His-GQD has a polygonal structure with the particle size of about 100 nm. The polygonal structure makes the exposure of high-index facets of Ag nanoparticles, which brings better catalytic ability. The HRTEM image of Ag/His-GQD indicates the lattice fringes of the (111) with a d-spacing of 0.235 nm (Fig. 1D), corresponding to monoclinic Ag nanocrystal. On the XRD pattern (Fig. 1E), there are eight peaks at 38.4°, 44.5°, 64.7°, 77.5°, 81.7°, 97.9°, 110.6°, and 114.9°, corresponding to (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331) and (420) planes of Ag nanocrystals, respectively. The diffraction peaks of all Ag nanocrystals are sharp, indicating highly crystalline nature. The XRD pattern is exactly matched with that of Ag (JCPDS NO. 04–0783), verifying that our synthesis achieves to the formation of Ag nanocrystals. Compared with the H-Ag/His-GQD, the Ag/His-GQD exhibits an increased diffraction intensities of the high-index facets, such as (220), (311), (222), (400), (331) and (420) facets. This indicates that the introduction of His-GQDs can improve the exposure of high-index facets of Ag nanocrystals, leading to an improved catalytic activity. On the IR spectrum (Fig. s4), the infrared spectra of His-GQDs and Ag/His-GQD were very similar. The absorption band of 3300–3600 cm−1 is attributed to symmetric stretching vibration of the O–H and N–H bonds. The absorption peak at 1690 cm−1 is caused by the stretch vibration of -C = O bond. The peak at 1589 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of C = N bond in the imidazole ring of histidine groups. The peak at 1349 cm−1 is stretching vibration C–H bond. The absorption peak at 1159 cm−1 is due to stretching vibration of the C-N bond. While it was not hard to see that the intensity of -C = O at 1690 cm−1 in the Ag/His-GQD spectra decreased. [26] This change is attributed to the interaction between the Ag nanoparticles and the oxygen-containing groups on His-GQDs, thus indicating that Ag nanoparticles have been combined with the His-GQDs.

Chemical composition of the Ag/His-GQD was studied by XPS technique. As shown in Fig. s5A, the Ag/His-GQD is composed of Ag, N, and C elements. N and C elements come from His-GQD. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. s5B) can be divided into four distinguishable peaks at 284.8, 286.2, 288, and 289.2 eV, corresponding to the C–C, C–O, C = O, and O–C = O groups. In the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. s5C), the peaks at 398.8, 400 and 401.3 eV were assigned to pyridinic, amine and graphitic N [27–28]. The high resolution spectrum of Ag 3d had two characteristics bands at 368 and 374 eV, corresponding to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 [29].

To evaluate the oxidase-like activity of Ag/His-GQD, the oxidation of TMB was selected as a model reaction. In the absence of H2O2, the Ag/His-GQD can catalyze the oxidation of TMB into blue product due to its oxidase-like activity. Fig. 2
A shows in the absense of TMB the Ag/His-GQD solution has only a wide absorption band at 445 nm, which should be the surface plasmon induction of Ag nanoparticles. While single TMB has no obvious absorption peak at 652 nm. However, the reaction system will rapidly change from colorless to deep blue when the Ag/His-GQD was added. The result demonstrates that Ag/His-GQD has a high oxidase-like activity. To study on the kinetic behavior of the catalytic reaction, effect of the reaction time on the absorbance at 652 nm (A652) for different reaction systems was investigated. As shown in Fig. s6, in the absence of Ag/His-GQD the A652 will slightly increase with the increase of reaction time. This verifies the TMB is difficult to be oxdized in air without catalyst. In the presence of Ag/His-GQD, the A652 will rapidly increase with increasing reaction time, indicating a good catalytic activity. The A652 reaches to its maximum value at 20 min and then remain almost unchange with the increase of reaction time. This proves that the all TMB molecules were oxidized into blue product. Further, the catalytic behaviours of different catalysts were studied. Fig. 2B presents the UV–vis absorption spectra of the reaction system catalyzed by Ag/His-GQD, H-Ag/His-GQD, spherical Ag and His-GQD. It can be seen from Fig. 2B that the reaction catalyzed by His-GQD has a lower absorbance. The A652 is obviously higher than that without any catalyst in Fig. 2A. This proves that His-GQD can offer an oxidase-like activity. When His-GQD was replaced by spherical Ag nanoparticles, the A652 will increase. The result demonstrates that spherical Ag nanoparticles has a better catalytic activity. Interestingly, the reaction catalytzed by H-Ag/His-GQD exhibits an increased A652 compared with the reaction with sperical Ag nanoparticles or His-GQD. This is attributed to the synergy between Ag nanoparticle and His-GQD. The synergy improves the catalytic activity and leads to an increased A652. On the one hand, the introduction of His-GQD improves the stability and dispersibility of Ag nanoparticles. This effectively prevents the agglomeration of Ag nanoparticles and results in more active sites exposure, improving the ability to catalyze oxidation of TMB. On the other hand, the synergistic effect between the Ag nanoparticles and the imidazole groups on the His-GQDs can speed up the transfer of electrons, thereby improving the catalytic efficiency. In addition, the surface of His-GQDs contains a large number of -C = O and O = CO–. These groups can act as catalytically active sites and substrate binding sites, further improving the catalytic ability [30]. When the H-Ag/His-GQD was replaced by Ag/His-GQD, the A652 will further increase. This demonstrates that Ag/His-GQD can offer a better catalytic activity. The enhanced catalytic activity is due to a more exposed high-index crystal facets of polygonal Ag nanoparticles in the Ag/His-GQD.

To understand the catalytic mechanism of Ag/His-GQD towards the oxidation of TMB, the absorbance spectra of the reaction systems saturated with air, N2 and O2 were measured and the results were shown in Fig. s7A. In the reaction system saturated N2, the absorbance is very small. The absorbance is very lower than that of the reaction system saturated O2. The result demonstrates that the dissolved oxygen plays a important role in the catalytic reaction [31]. To further prove the possible intermediates (hydroxyl radicals, superoxide radicals) in the catalytic TMB oxidation process, the free radical scavengers were added to the reaction system for studying on the influence on the absorbance at 652 nm. As shown in Fig. s7B, when terephthalic acid as a hydroxyl radical inhibitor was added to the system, the A652 has barely changed, indicating that there was not large amount of hydroxyl radicals (·OH) in the system. However, after adding p-benzoquinone as an inhibitor of oxygen free radicals to the system, the A652 obviously reduced, indicating the existence of oxygen free radicals in the system. Thus, the O2 dissolved in the buffer is first activated by Ag/His-GQD to generate superoxide free radicals, and then the free radicals generated with strong oxidation ability leads to the oxidation of TMB to blue product.

Like other mimetic enzyme, the oxidase-like activity of Ag/His-GQD may be affected by pH and temperature. In order to obtain a high oxidase-like activity, the oxidase-like activity of Ag/His-GQD was studied by mixing Ag/His-GQD at the temperatures from 10 to 60℃ and different pH from 3.6 to 5.2 of acetate buffers. As shown in Fig. s8, the oxidase-like activity of Ag/His-GQD reaches to the highest at 30℃ and pH 4. So, the temperature of 30℃ and pH of 4.0 were employed for the oxidation of TMB.

To further understand the reaction kinetics for the oxidation of TMB catalyzed by Ag/His-GQD, the steady-state kinetic analysis was carried out. The steady-state kinetic parameters of Ag/His-GQD are determined by Michaelis-Menten curve (Fig. s9A) and the corresponding double-reciprocal Lineweaver-Burk plot (Fig. s9B). The lower Km presents a higher affinity between the mimetic enzyme and the substrate. A higher catalytic efficiency of the substrate presents a higher νmax and a stronger the catalytic activity [32]. The value of Km is 0.6 mM, the νmax is 22.5 × 10-8 M s−1. Compared with other mimetic enzyme in Table 1 , the Ag/His-GQD has a better affinity to TMB. This may be due to the synergistic effect of Ag nanoparticle and His-GQD as well as the special polygonal structure of Ag nanoparticles. Compared with natural enzymes such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and laccase, the Km of Ag/His-GQD is higher than that of HRP and laccase, Ag/His-GQD nanozyme showed a higher νmax for TMB than that of HRP and laccase, indicating that Ag/His-GQD nanozyme effectively catalyzed the oxidation of TMB. And Ag/His-GQD nanozyme also shows the advantages of simple preparation, low cost, and excellent stability against harsh conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibitory effect of D-PA on the oxidase-like activity of Ag/His-GQD
ce:section.ce:para:
The constructed Ag/His-GQD mimetic enzyme colorimetric reaction system was used for determination of D-PA (Fig. 3
A). The Ag/His-GQD with high catalytic activity can catalyze the oxidation of TMB to produce blue product. While 0.5 μM D-PA was added to the Ag/His-GQD in the acetate buffer solution of pH 4, the sulfhydryl in D-PA was combined with the Ag/His-GQD via Ag-S bond, the active site of the Ag/His-GQD was blocked, resulting in a reduction in the oxidation rate of TMB, and the solution appears light blue. Thus, the constructed Ag/His-GQD mimetic enzyme colorimetric system can be used for detection of D-PA.

The constructed Ag/His-GQD mimetic enzyme colorimetric reaction system was used to determine D-PA under optimal conditions. Fig. 3B shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of different concentrations of D-PA in reaction system. It can be seen that the A652 gradually decreases with the increase of D-PA concentration in the range 0.1–1.25 μM. The linear regression equation is ΔA = 0.2052 [CD-PA, μM] + 0.0182 (ΔA = A0 − A, A0 and A represents the absorbance value of the reaction system at 652 nm in the absence and presence of D-PA, respectively), the correlation coefficient 0.9992, and the detection limit of 0.034 μM (LOD, S/N = 3). In addition, the sensitivity of this method was compared with other reported methods in Table 2 , which showed higher sensitivity. This is due to the special polygonal structure of Ag nanoparticles and the synergistic effect between Ag nanoparticles and His-GQD, which improves the catalytic activity of the Ag/His-GQD, thereby improving the sensitivity of D-PA determination.

The reproducibility of bath production is a very important factor for the used of mimetic enzyme. Three batches of Ag/His-GQD product were prepared by the same procedure for the catalytic oxidation of TMB. Fig. s10 shows that the variance in catalytic activity of three batches of Ag/His-GQD products is 4.46%, showing a good reproducibility. The storage stability of Ag/His-GQD was also evaluated by measuring the catalytic activity. The relative activity of Ag/His-GQD remained at 97.4% for 15 days (Fig. s11). The results showed that Ag/His-GQD had good storage stability.

The selectivity of proposed Ag/His-GQD mimetic enzyme based colorimetric assay for detecting 0.5 μM D-PA was studied. The effects of common excipients in tablets on the determination of D-PA were investigated, such as sucrose, lactose, glucose, starch, SDS, K+, Na+, and Ca2+. After adding these excipients to the reaction system, respectively, the A652 was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer. The results showed that those excipients had little effect on D-PA detection (Fig. 4 ), which proved that the proposed method has excellent selectivity for D-PA.

To evaluate the feasibility of Ag/His-GQD mimetic enzyme-based colorimetric assay, the proposed method was applied to determine D-PA in the medicine samples. The samples were purchased from Wuxi Pharmacy. 5 tablets were taken out, ground into powder and mixed. An appropriate amount sample was taken out and dissolved in 250 mL doubly distilled water. After filtering through a 0.45 μm filter membrane, the obtained filtrate was used as the stock solution for D-PA determination. Table 3 shows the D-PA recoveries are between 93 and 98%. Therefore, this method can be used for determination of D-PA in medicines.

The study has demonstrated synthesis of silver nanocrystals using histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots as a chelating agent and shape inducer. The resulted nanohybrid shows more exposed high-index facets and excellent oxidase-like activity. The silver nanocrystal was designed as a nanoenzyme for colorimetric detection of D-penicillamine. The proposed method exhibits a higher sensitivity, selectivity and stability compared with the present analytical methods for D-penicillamine. The study also provides a promising approach for fabrication of various metal nanocrystals with excellent catalytic activity.
Xu Dan: Investigation. Li Ruiyi: Methodology. Wang Qinsheng: Investigation. Yang Yongqiang: Visualization, Investigation. Wang Guangli: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1603001), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX20-1776), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute (KJ(Y)2020037), Emergency response to COVID-19 washing products of Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education (No. YJ2020-02) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106204.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
